metadata.title: Azure DevOps Engineer AZ-400 Practice Questions

3.name: Design and Implement Build and Release Pipelines

4.name: Develop a Security and Compliance Plan

questions.question: Which Azure DevOps service provides Kanban boards, backlogs, and sprint planning for Agile teams?
questions.explanation: Azure Boards provides Kanban boards, backlogs, sprint planning, and work item tracking for Agile project management within Azure DevOps.

questions.question: What is the default work item type used to track user requirements in Azure Boards with the Agile process template?
questions.explanation: In the Agile process template, User Story is the default work item type for tracking user requirements. Scrum uses Product Backlog Item, and CMMI uses Requirement.

questions.question: Which Azure DevOps feature allows teams to create and share documentation within the project?
questions.explanation: Azure DevOps Wiki provides a built-in documentation platform where teams can create, edit, and share Markdown-based documentation within the project.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a Dashboard in Azure DevOps?
options: To display widgets showing project metrics, build status, work item charts, and team progress
questions.explanation: Azure DevOps Dashboards display configurable widgets that visualize project metrics, build/release status, work item queries, burndown charts, and other team performance data.

questions.question: Which notification type in Azure DevOps can automatically alert team members when a work item is assigned to them?
questions.explanation: Azure DevOps notifications can be configured as personal or team subscriptions that automatically alert members via email when specific events occur, such as work item assignment changes.

questions.question: Which process template in Azure Boards provides the most formal and structured approach, designed for organizations requiring CMMI compliance?
questions.explanation: The CMMI process template provides the most formal structure with work item types including Requirements, Change Requests, and Reviews, designed for organizations following CMMI practices.

questions.question: What is a Sprint in Azure Boards?
options: A time-boxed iteration where the team commits to completing a set of work items
questions.explanation: A Sprint (iteration) is a time-boxed period (typically 1-4 weeks) where the team selects and commits to completing a set of prioritized work items from the backlog.

questions.question: A team needs to track work across multiple sprints and visualize dependencies between features. Which Azure Boards feature provides this cross-sprint visibility?
questions.explanation: Delivery Plans provide a calendar view across multiple teams and sprints, showing work item schedules and dependencies, enabling portfolio-level planning visibility.

questions.question: A project manager needs to enforce that all bugs must have a severity field filled in before they can be moved to the Active state. Which feature should they configure?
options: Custom rules on work item types (state transition rules)
questions.explanation: Custom rules on work item types can enforce field requirements during state transitions. You can configure rules that require specific fields to be filled before allowing a work item to move to a particular state.

questions.question: A team wants to integrate Azure DevOps notifications with their Microsoft Teams channel so that build failures are posted automatically. What should they configure?
options: Azure DevOps service hooks connected to Microsoft Teams
questions.explanation: Service hooks in Azure DevOps can send event notifications to external services like Microsoft Teams, Slack, or webhooks when events like build failures or work item changes occur.

questions.question: A team is using the Scrum process template but needs additional custom fields on their Product Backlog Items to track business value and risk score. What should they do?
options: Create an inherited process and add custom fields to the Product Backlog Item work item type
Switch to the CMMI process template
Add comments to each work item manually
Create a separate Excel spreadsheet to track these values
questions.explanation: Azure DevOps allows creating inherited processes from the base templates where you can add custom fields, rules, and pages to work item types without modifying the original process.

questions.question: A distributed team needs a shared knowledge base that is version-controlled and supports Markdown with embedded diagrams. Which Azure DevOps Wiki option should they use?
questions.explanation: Publishing code as wiki creates a repo-backed wiki that is fully version-controlled through Git, supports Markdown with Mermaid diagrams, and allows the team to manage documentation using standard Git workflows.

questions.question: A product owner wants to visualize the team's velocity over the last 6 sprints to forecast capacity for upcoming sprints. Which Azure Boards feature provides this?
questions.explanation: The Velocity widget displays completed work (in story points or count) per sprint, allowing product owners to track velocity trends and forecast capacity for upcoming iterations.

questions.question: A large organization has 10 teams working on the same product. They need a unified backlog view that allows prioritization across all teams. What should they configure?
options: Portfolio backlogs with Features and Epics that link to team-level work items
questions.explanation: Portfolio backlogs (Epics and Features) provide a hierarchical backlog structure where leadership can prioritize at the portfolio level while individual teams manage their own sprint backlogs.

questions.question: A team wants to create custom dashboard widgets that pull data from external systems. What is the recommended approach?
options: Develop a custom widget extension using the Azure DevOps Extension SDK
Use only the built-in widgets provided by Azure DevOps
Embed an external website in an iframe widget
Create a Power BI report and screenshot it
questions.explanation: The Azure DevOps Extension SDK allows developers to create custom dashboard widgets that can query external data sources, display custom visualizations, and integrate with the dashboard framework.

questions.question: An enterprise is migrating from Jira to Azure Boards. They have 50,000 work items with custom fields, attachments, and history. What is the recommended migration approach?
options: Use the Azure DevOps Migration Tools (open-source) or a third-party migration tool that maps Jira fields to Azure Boards work item fields
Manually re-create all work items in Azure Boards
Export Jira to CSV and import using the Azure DevOps CSV import feature
Abandon historical data and start fresh
questions.explanation: The Azure DevOps Migration Tools (open-source by Martin Hinshelwood) or commercial tools like OpsHub provide field mapping, history preservation, and attachment migration for large-scale Jira-to-Azure Boards migrations.

questions.question: A regulated industry team needs to enforce that every work item state change is auditable, with traceability from requirement to code change to test result. Which combination of Azure DevOps features provides end-to-end traceability?
options: Work item linking (parent/child, related), branch policies requiring work item association, and Azure Test Plans linked to requirements
questions.explanation: End-to-end traceability is achieved by linking work items hierarchically, requiring branch policies that associate commits/PRs with work items, and linking test cases to requirements in Azure Test Plans.

questions.question: A DevOps team needs to implement automated work item management where completing all child tasks automatically moves the parent User Story to a 'Resolved' state. How should they implement this?
options: Configure custom rules with child work item state rollup triggers on the inherited process
Use an Azure Function triggered by webhook to update parent items
Train all team members to manually update parent items
Use the Kanban board auto-state feature for all item levels
questions.explanation: Custom rules in inherited processes can be configured with conditions based on child work item states, automatically transitioning the parent work item when all children reach a specified state.

questions.question: A team lead wants to create a Cumulative Flow Diagram (CFD) to identify bottlenecks in their development process. What does a widening band in the 'In Review' column indicate?
options: Work is accumulating in the review stage faster than it is being completed, indicating a bottleneck
The team is completing reviews faster than expected
New work items are being added to the backlog
The sprint is ahead of schedule
questions.explanation: A widening band in a CFD indicates that items are entering that stage faster than they are leaving. In the 'In Review' column, this means reviews are a bottleneck that needs attention.

questions.question: An organization needs to manage stakeholder feedback by collecting feature requests from external users, automatically routing them to the appropriate team backlog, and providing status updates. Which approach best integrates with Azure DevOps?
options: Azure DevOps service hooks with a custom feedback portal that creates work items via the REST API, using area paths for team routing
A shared email inbox monitored by a project manager
A GitHub Issues repository mirrored to Azure Boards
Manual entry of feedback into Azure Boards by an administrator
questions.explanation: Using the Azure DevOps REST API to create work items from a custom feedback portal, combined with area path-based team routing and service hooks for status notifications, provides automated stakeholder feedback management.

questions.question: What chart shows the remaining work in a sprint over time?
questions.explanation: A burndown chart shows the remaining work (in story points, hours, or count) plotted against time within a sprint, helping the team track whether they are on pace to complete all committed work.

questions.question: How can a team share query results with all project members in Azure Boards?
options: Email the query results to all members
Export to CSV and upload to SharePoint
Create a personal query and share the URL
questions.explanation: Shared Queries in Azure Boards are accessible to all project members. You can organize them in folders with appropriate permissions for different teams.

questions.question: A team needs to implement WIP (Work in Progress) limits on their Kanban board to prevent overloading team members. If the 'Development' column has a WIP limit of 5 and 5 items are already there, what happens when someone tries to move a 6th item in?
options: The column header changes color to warn that the WIP limit is exceeded, but the move is still allowed
The oldest item is automatically moved to the next column
An email notification is sent to the team lead
questions.explanation: Azure Boards Kanban WIP limits are advisory by default. When exceeded, the column header changes color to highlight the violation, but the team can still move items. This encourages discussion about why the limit was exceeded.

questions.question: A team wants to generate weekly status reports that include sprint progress, open bugs, and build success rates. What is the most efficient Azure DevOps approach?
options: Create a dashboard with Burndown, Query Results, and Build History widgets, and share the dashboard link
Manually compile data from each service into a Word document
Use Azure Monitor for DevOps metrics
Export each board to CSV and merge in Excel
questions.explanation: Azure DevOps dashboards with configurable widgets provide real-time visibility into sprint progress, bug trends, and build metrics, eliminating the need for manual report compilation.

questions.question: An organization with multiple projects wants to implement consistent process governance. They need all projects to use the same work item types and custom fields. What is the best approach?
options: Create a single custom inherited process and apply it to all projects in the organization
Configure each project independently with the same settings
Use Azure Policy to enforce process compliance
Create a template project and clone it for each new project
questions.explanation: A custom inherited process defined at the organization level can be applied to multiple projects, ensuring consistent work item types, fields, rules, and states across the entire organization.

questions.question: A team needs to automate their release notes generation from work items completed in each sprint. Which approach integrates best with Azure DevOps?
options: Use the Azure DevOps REST API to query closed work items by iteration path and generate formatted release notes in the pipeline
Manually write release notes after each sprint
Export the sprint board to PDF
questions.explanation: The Azure DevOps REST API can query work items by iteration path and state, allowing automated generation of formatted release notes as a pipeline task, with links back to work items for traceability.

questions.question: Which Azure Boards metric measures the time from when a work item is created to when it is completed?
questions.explanation: Lead time measures the total elapsed time from work item creation to completion, including time spent waiting in the backlog. Cycle time measures only the time from when active work begins.

questions.question: A team wants to enforce that high-priority bugs are addressed before new feature work can begin in a sprint. Which Kanban board configuration helps enforce this?
options: Configure swimlanes to separate 'Expedite' items from regular work with a dedicated high-priority lane
Set WIP limits to 0 on all feature columns
Create a separate board for bugs only
Use tags to highlight priority bugs
questions.explanation: Kanban swimlanes allow teams to create an 'Expedite' lane for high-priority items that must be addressed first, providing visual separation and workflow priority enforcement.

questions.question: A team needs to receive real-time notifications in Slack when a pull request is created or completed. Which Azure DevOps feature enables this?
questions.explanation: Service hooks in Azure DevOps can trigger events to external services like Slack. You can configure hooks for pull request created, completed, and other code events to post to specific Slack channels.

questions.question: An organization is implementing SAFe (Scaled Agile Framework) with Azure DevOps. They need to manage Program Increments, features across multiple Agile Release Trains, and PI planning boards. What configuration is required?
options: Configure Delivery Plans for cross-team planning, use Epics for PI objectives, Features for capabilities, and Area Paths for team routing within a single project
Create separate Azure DevOps organizations for each Agile Release Train
Use a third-party SAFe tool with no Azure DevOps integration
Use only the Basic process template across all teams
questions.explanation: SAFe implementation in Azure DevOps uses Delivery Plans for PI planning visibility, the Epic-Feature-Story hierarchy for portfolio management, and Area Paths to route work to specific teams within a single project.

questions.question: Which distributed version control system is the default source control provider in Azure Repos?
questions.explanation: Git is the default and recommended distributed version control system in Azure Repos, providing branching, merging, and full local repository capabilities.

questions.question: What is a Pull Request (PR) in Azure Repos?
options: A request to merge changes from one branch into another, enabling code review before merging
A request to pull code from a remote repository
A request to delete a branch
A request to create a new repository
questions.explanation: A Pull Request in Azure Repos is a mechanism for proposing changes from a source branch to a target branch, enabling code review, discussion, and policy validation before merging.

questions.question: Which branching strategy creates feature branches off the main branch and merges back after completion?
questions.explanation: GitHub Flow is a simple branching strategy where developers create feature branches from main, make changes, open a pull request for review, and merge back to main upon approval.

questions.question: What does 'git clone' do?
options: Creates a local copy of a remote repository
questions.explanation: The 'git clone' command creates a complete local copy of a remote Git repository, including all branches, commits, and history.

questions.question: Which Git command combines changes from one branch into another by creating a merge commit?
questions.explanation: The 'git merge' command integrates changes from one branch into another, creating a merge commit that preserves the complete branch history.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a .gitignore file?
options: To specify files and directories that Git should not track
To list team members who can access the repository
questions.explanation: The .gitignore file specifies patterns for files and directories that Git should ignore (not track), such as build outputs, dependencies, and sensitive configuration files.

questions.question: In the GitFlow branching strategy, which branch contains the production-ready code?
questions.explanation: In GitFlow, the main (master) branch always reflects the production-ready state. The develop branch serves as the integration branch for features.

questions.question: A team needs to enforce that at least two reviewers approve every PR before it can be merged to the main branch. Which Azure Repos feature should they configure?
options: Branch policies with minimum number of reviewers
questions.explanation: Branch policies in Azure Repos can require a minimum number of reviewer approvals before a pull request can be completed, enforcing code review standards.

questions.question: A developer wants to incorporate recent changes from the main branch into their feature branch while maintaining a linear commit history. Which Git operation should they use?
questions.explanation: Rebasing replays the feature branch commits on top of the latest main branch, creating a linear commit history without merge commits. This makes the history cleaner and easier to read.

questions.question: A team wants to ensure that all PRs pass a build validation before they can be merged. Which branch policy should they enable?
options: Build validation policy (trigger a build pipeline on PR creation/update)
Require a minimum number of reviewers
questions.explanation: The build validation branch policy triggers a specified build pipeline whenever a PR is created or updated, and prevents merging until the build succeeds.

questions.question: What is the advantage of squash merging a feature branch into main?
options: All commits from the feature branch are combined into a single commit, keeping the main branch history clean
It preserves every individual commit from the feature branch
It creates a merge commit with two parents
It prevents any future branch creation
questions.explanation: Squash merge combines all commits from a feature branch into a single commit on the target branch, keeping the main branch history clean and concise without granular feature branch commits.

questions.question: A repository has grown to 10 GB due to large binary files that were committed historically. What is the recommended solution to manage large files in Git?
options: Git Large File Storage (LFS) to store large files as pointers with the actual content in external storage
Split the repository into multiple smaller repositories
Delete the repository and start fresh
questions.explanation: Git LFS replaces large files with lightweight pointers in the repository while storing the actual file content on a separate server, keeping the repository size manageable.

questions.question: A team decides to use trunk-based development. What characterizes this branching strategy?
options: Developers commit directly to main or use very short-lived feature branches (less than a day), with feature flags for incomplete features
Long-lived feature branches merged after weeks of development
Separate branches for each environment (dev, staging, prod)
A develop branch that collects features before releasing
questions.explanation: Trunk-based development focuses on a single main branch with very short-lived branches (hours, not days). Feature flags are used to hide incomplete features, enabling continuous integration.

questions.question: Which Azure Repos branch policy ensures that code review comments are addressed before a PR can be completed?
questions.explanation: The 'Require comment resolution' branch policy prevents a pull request from being completed until all comments have been resolved, ensuring feedback is addressed before merging.

questions.question: A developer needs to apply a specific commit from one branch to another without merging the entire branch. Which Git command should they use?
questions.explanation: The 'git cherry-pick' command applies the changes from a specific commit to the current branch, allowing selective application of individual commits between branches.

questions.question: An organization is deciding between a mono-repo and multi-repo strategy for 15 microservices. The services share common libraries and the team wants atomic cross-service changes. Which strategy is more appropriate?
options: Mono-repo, because it enables atomic commits across services and simplifies sharing common libraries
Multi-repo, because each service should be independent
One repo per team regardless of service count
A fork-based workflow with a separate repo per developer
questions.explanation: A mono-repo allows atomic commits that span multiple services and shared libraries, simplifying dependency management and cross-service refactoring. The tradeoff is build complexity and larger repository size.

questions.question: A team needs to recover a commit that was accidentally removed during a force push. Which Git feature allows them to find and restore the lost commit?
options: git reflog to find the lost commit hash, then git reset or cherry-pick to restore it
git log to search for the commit
Contact Azure DevOps support to restore the branch
Restore from a backup of the repository
questions.explanation: Git reflog records all reference changes (including force pushes and rebases) locally. It can be used to find the hash of a 'lost' commit, which can then be recovered via git cherry-pick or git reset.

questions.question: A security-conscious organization needs to prevent developers from pushing secrets (API keys, passwords) to Azure Repos. Which approach provides automated pre-push protection?
options: Configure credential scanning in the CI pipeline and enable push policies that run a secret detection tool (e.g., CredScan or GitLeaks) on every push
Train developers to be careful with secrets
Use branch permissions to restrict pushes
questions.explanation: Automated credential scanning tools like GitLeaks or Microsoft CredScan can be configured as pre-receive hooks or CI pipeline tasks to detect and block pushes containing secrets or credentials.

questions.question: A team is experiencing frequent merge conflicts in a shared configuration file. What is the best strategy to reduce these conflicts?
options: Break the monolithic configuration file into smaller, team-specific files and use Git merge drivers for structured formats
Lock the file so only one person can edit it at a time
Use a separate branch for each edit
Rewrite the file format to JSON
questions.explanation: Splitting a monolithic config file into smaller, logically separated files reduces the likelihood of concurrent edits touching the same lines. Custom merge drivers can handle structured format merges automatically.

questions.question: An architect needs to implement a Git repository strategy that allows multiple teams to work independently while sharing common components. Each team needs its own release cycle. What approach should they use?
options: Multi-repo with shared components published as packages (Azure Artifacts), consumed by each team's repository
A single mono-repo with team-specific branches
Forking the main repository for each team
Git submodules referencing a shared repo
questions.explanation: Publishing shared components as packages to Azure Artifacts allows each team to have independent repos and release cycles while consuming shared libraries as versioned dependencies.

questions.question: A team wants to enforce that their main branch only contains linear history (no merge commits). Which combination of Azure Repos settings achieves this?
options: Branch policy requiring squash merge or rebase merge strategy for all PRs
Require all developers to use 'git push --force'
Configure a post-merge hook to rewrite history
questions.explanation: Branch policies can restrict the allowed merge strategies to squash merge or rebase merge only, which both produce a linear commit history on the target branch without merge commits.

questions.question: Which Azure Repos feature allows you to include one Git repository inside another as a subdirectory?
questions.explanation: Git submodules allow you to include and track an external Git repository as a subdirectory within your repository, useful for including shared libraries or dependencies.

questions.question: A team needs to migrate from TFVC (Team Foundation Version Control) to Git in Azure Repos while preserving full change history. What is the recommended approach?
options: Use the git-tfs tool to import TFVC history into a Git repository, then push to Azure Repos
Copy all files manually into a new Git repo (losing history)
Use Azure DevOps built-in import feature (tip migration only)
Export TFVC changeset reports and attach to the Git repo wiki
questions.explanation: The git-tfs tool provides full-fidelity TFVC-to-Git migration preserving complete changeset history, branch structure, and metadata. Azure DevOps built-in import provides tip-only migration.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a 'release branch' in the GitFlow strategy?
options: To stabilize the code for an upcoming release, allowing only bug fixes while feature development continues on develop
To deploy code directly to production
questions.explanation: In GitFlow, a release branch is created from develop to stabilize the code before a release. Only bug fixes are allowed on the release branch while new features continue on develop.

questions.question: An organization needs to implement code ownership where specific teams must review changes to files in their domain. How should they configure this in Azure Repos?
options: Configure automatic reviewers in branch policies based on file path patterns (CODEOWNERS-style)
Manually assign reviewers to every PR
questions.explanation: Azure Repos branch policies support automatic reviewer assignment based on file path patterns, similar to GitHub CODEOWNERS. When files matching a pattern are changed, designated reviewers are automatically required.

questions.question: A developer accidentally committed a large binary file (500 MB) to the repository history. Simply deleting the file in a new commit does not reduce the repo size. How should they permanently remove it from history?
options: Use git filter-repo or BFG Repo Cleaner to rewrite history and remove the large file from all commits
Delete the file and force push
Create a new repository and copy only the latest files
Use git gc to clean up the repository
questions.explanation: git filter-repo (or BFG Repo Cleaner) rewrites the repository history to permanently remove large files from all commits, followed by a force push to update the remote repository.

questions.question: What is a fork in the context of Azure Repos?
options: A server-side copy of a repository that allows independent development without affecting the original
A branch within the same repository
questions.explanation: A fork creates a server-side copy of a repository under a different namespace, allowing independent development. Changes can be proposed back to the original repository via pull requests.

questions.question: When should a team use a hotfix branch in the GitFlow strategy?
options: When a critical bug in production needs an immediate fix that cannot wait for the next planned release
When adding a new feature to production
When refactoring code on the develop branch
When merging the develop branch to main
questions.explanation: Hotfix branches are created from main to fix critical production bugs immediately. After the fix, they are merged into both main (for production) and develop (to include the fix in future releases).

questions.question: A team wants to automatically link Azure Boards work items to commits and PRs. Which practice ensures this linkage?
options: Include the work item ID in commit messages (e.g., 'AB#1234') and enable the branch policy requiring linked work items on PRs
Manually link work items after each commit
Use tags on branches that match work item IDs
Create a branch named after the work item
questions.explanation: Including 'AB#<workItemId>' in commit messages automatically links the commit to the Azure Boards work item. Branch policies can additionally require that PRs have at least one linked work item.

questions.question: A large team uses feature flags to enable trunk-based development. Which approach ensures that stale feature flags are cleaned up after features are fully released?
options: Track feature flags as work items with a defined lifecycle, and include flag removal as a Definition of Done criterion for the feature
Leave all flags in the codebase permanently
Delete flags manually at the end of each quarter
Use runtime configuration only, never code-level flags
questions.explanation: Managing feature flags as tracked work items with a defined lifecycle ensures accountability. Including flag removal in the Definition of Done prevents stale flags from accumulating in the codebase.

questions.question: Which Azure DevOps service provides CI/CD pipeline capabilities?
questions.explanation: Azure Pipelines provides continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) capabilities, supporting build, test, and deployment automation for any language and platform.

questions.question: What is the file format used to define Azure Pipelines in code?
questions.explanation: Azure Pipelines uses YAML files (typically azure-pipelines.yml) to define build and release pipelines as code, enabling version control and review of pipeline definitions.

questions.question: What is an Azure Pipelines 'agent'?
options: A compute resource that runs pipeline jobs, either Microsoft-hosted or self-hosted
questions.explanation: An agent is a compute environment where pipeline jobs execute. Microsoft-hosted agents are managed by Azure DevOps, while self-hosted agents run on your own infrastructure.

questions.question: What triggers a CI (Continuous Integration) pipeline?
options: A code commit or push to a specified branch
A manual approval from a manager
questions.explanation: CI pipelines are typically triggered automatically when code is committed or pushed to specified branches, running builds and tests to validate changes immediately.

questions.question: Which Azure DevOps service provides a universal package management solution for NuGet, npm, Maven, and Python packages?
questions.explanation: Azure Artifacts provides package management for NuGet, npm, Maven, Python (pip), and universal packages, allowing teams to create, host, and share packages within the organization.

questions.question: In YAML pipelines, what is a 'stage'?
options: A logical grouping of related jobs that can represent a phase like Build, Test, or Deploy
A single task within a job
questions.explanation: A stage in YAML pipelines is a logical boundary that groups related jobs. Common stages include Build, Test, and Deploy. Stages can have dependencies and approval gates.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a deployment 'environment' in Azure Pipelines?
options: A named target for deployments that can have approval checks, resource locks, and deployment history
A set of environment variables for the build
A virtual machine image for agents
questions.explanation: Environments in Azure Pipelines represent deployment targets (dev, staging, production) and can be configured with approval gates, exclusive lock checks, and deployment history tracking.

questions.question: A team wants to deploy an updated web application to 10% of their production servers first, monitor for errors, and then gradually roll out to the remaining servers. Which deployment strategy should they use?
questions.explanation: Canary deployment gradually rolls out changes to a small subset of users/servers first, allowing monitoring for issues before expanding the deployment to the full production environment.

questions.question: A multi-stage YAML pipeline needs to pass build artifacts from the Build stage to the Deploy stage. How should this be implemented?
options: Use the 'publish' task to upload artifacts in the Build stage and 'download' task to retrieve them in the Deploy stage
Store artifacts in a shared file system
Rebuild the code in the Deploy stage
Use pipeline variables to pass file contents
questions.explanation: Pipeline artifacts are published using the 'PublishPipelineArtifact' task in the Build stage and downloaded using 'DownloadPipelineArtifact' in subsequent stages, providing artifact passing between stages.

questions.question: A team wants to run the same deployment pipeline across Development, Staging, and Production environments with different configurations. What is the recommended YAML approach?
options: Use pipeline templates with parameters and variable groups per environment
Use if/else conditions for every task
Hard-code environment-specific values in the YAML file
questions.explanation: YAML templates with parameters allow reusing deployment logic across environments, while variable groups provide environment-specific configuration values, following the DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) principle.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of a blue-green deployment strategy?
options: Near-zero downtime with instant rollback by switching traffic between two identical production environments
Gradual traffic shifting based on percentage
questions.explanation: Blue-green deployment maintains two identical environments (blue=current, green=new). Traffic is switched from blue to green after validation, with instant rollback by switching back to blue if issues arise.

questions.question: A pipeline needs to run different sets of tests in parallel to reduce overall execution time. How should this be configured in YAML?
options: Define multiple jobs within the same stage without dependencies between them
Run all tests sequentially in a single job
Create separate pipelines for each test suite
Use scheduled triggers to stagger test runs
questions.explanation: Multiple jobs within a stage that have no dependency on each other run in parallel by default on separate agents, significantly reducing total pipeline execution time.

questions.question: A team needs to ensure that production deployments are approved by a designated release manager before they proceed. Which Azure Pipelines feature should they use?
options: Environment approvals and checks (manual approval gate)
questions.explanation: Environment approvals in Azure Pipelines require designated approvers to manually approve before a deployment stage targeting that environment can proceed, providing a human gate for production deployments.

questions.question: What is an Azure Artifacts 'upstream source'?
options: A connection to a public registry (like nuget.org or npmjs.com) that allows packages to be cached in your private feed
A Git repository hosting package source code
A deployment target for package releases
A build agent that produces packages
questions.explanation: Upstream sources in Azure Artifacts connect your private feed to public registries, caching packages on first use and providing a single source for both private and public packages.

questions.question: A team needs to cache npm dependencies between pipeline runs to speed up builds. Which pipeline feature should they use?
options: Pipeline caching (Cache task) with the node_modules folder and package-lock.json as the cache key
Publishing node_modules as a pipeline artifact
Using a self-hosted agent with a persistent workspace
questions.explanation: The Cache task in Azure Pipelines stores and restores specified directories (like node_modules) between runs using a hash key (like package-lock.json), significantly reducing dependency installation time.

questions.question: A company uses Azure Pipelines to deploy to Azure App Service. They want zero-downtime deployments. Which App Service feature should the pipeline leverage?
questions.explanation: Deployment slots allow deploying to a staging slot, warming it up, and then swapping it with the production slot, achieving zero-downtime deployment with instant rollback capability.

questions.question: A DevOps team needs to implement a deployment strategy where a new version is deployed alongside the old version, and traffic is gradually shifted using weighted routing. Which combination achieves this in Azure?
options: Azure Traffic Manager with weighted routing or Azure Front Door with traffic split, deployed via Azure Pipelines
Azure Application Gateway with URL routing
Azure DNS with multiple A records
questions.explanation: Traffic Manager weighted routing or Front Door traffic split allows gradual traffic shifting between old and new deployments, controlled by pipeline tasks that adjust weights during canary rollouts.

questions.question: A team needs to implement a package versioning strategy that prevents breaking changes from affecting consumers. Their packages are consumed by multiple teams. Which versioning approach should they follow?
options: Semantic Versioning (SemVer) with MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH, incrementing MAJOR for breaking changes
questions.explanation: Semantic Versioning (SemVer) communicates the nature of changes: MAJOR for breaking changes, MINOR for backward-compatible features, and PATCH for backward-compatible fixes, enabling consumers to manage risk.

questions.question: A DevOps engineer needs to create reusable pipeline components that can be shared across multiple repositories and teams. Which YAML feature provides this?
options: Template files stored in a shared repository and referenced using 'resources: repositories' and 'template' keyword
questions.explanation: YAML templates in a shared repository can be referenced by other pipelines using 'resources: repositories', enabling centralized management of reusable pipeline components across the organization.

questions.question: A team needs their deployment pipeline to automatically rollback to the previous version if health checks fail within 10 minutes of deployment. How should this be implemented?
options: Use a post-deployment gate that runs health check queries against Azure Monitor, with a rollback stage triggered on failure
Manually monitor the application and trigger rollback if needed
Set an Azure Alert to notify the team via email
Always deploy to a staging slot and never swap to production
questions.explanation: Post-deployment gates can evaluate Azure Monitor queries and invoke a rollback stage automatically if health checks fail within the configured evaluation period, enabling automated recovery.

questions.question: An organization needs to promote packages through different quality stages: development, testing, release-ready. Which Azure Artifacts feature supports this workflow?
options: Feed views (e.g., @Local, @Prerelease, @Release) that represent quality levels
questions.explanation: Azure Artifacts feed views represent quality levels. Packages start in @Local, are promoted to @Prerelease after testing, and to @Release when production-ready, providing a package promotion workflow.

questions.question: A DevOps team needs to implement Infrastructure as Code (IaC) with drift detection. The pipeline should deploy infrastructure changes and fail if manual changes were made outside the pipeline. Which approach achieves this?
options: Use Terraform with a plan stage that detects drift, fails on unexpected changes, and an apply stage that requires approval
Deploy ARM templates without a what-if check
Use Azure CLI scripts for all infrastructure changes
Manually review the Azure portal before each deployment
questions.explanation: Terraform plan detects drift between the desired state (code) and actual state (infrastructure), showing unexpected changes. The pipeline can fail on drift, and Terraform apply enforces the desired state after approval.

questions.question: A pipeline needs to deploy to multiple Azure regions sequentially, waiting for health validation in each region before proceeding to the next. How should the YAML pipeline be structured?
options: Use multiple deployment stages with dependencies, each targeting a regional environment with approval and health check gates
Deploy to all regions simultaneously in a single stage
Use a single deployment stage with a loop over regions
questions.explanation: Sequential deployment stages with dependencies ensure each region is deployed and validated before proceeding. Environment-level approval gates and health check gates provide the validation checkpoints.

questions.question: A team needs to build and push Docker images as part of their CI pipeline, storing images in Azure Container Registry. Which pipeline task and authentication method should they use?
options: Docker@2 task with an Azure Container Registry service connection using a service principal
Bash script with docker login using hardcoded credentials
PowerShell script with az acr login
Manual docker push after the pipeline completes
questions.explanation: The Docker@2 task provides built-in support for building and pushing images to Azure Container Registry, using a service connection with a service principal for secure, credential-free authentication.

questions.question: What is the benefit of using a self-hosted agent over a Microsoft-hosted agent in Azure Pipelines?
options: Persistent workspace caching, custom tool installations, and access to private network resources
questions.explanation: Self-hosted agents provide persistent workspace caching between builds, custom tool/SDK installations, access to private networks, and control over the hardware and OS configuration.

questions.question: A DevOps engineer needs to implement a pipeline that runs integration tests against an ephemeral environment that is created before testing and destroyed afterward. Which YAML feature supports this?
options: Deployment jobs with lifecycle hooks (preDeploy, deploy, postDeploy, cleanup) using IaC to create and destroy the environment
Running tests on the production environment during off-hours
Manual environment provisioning before pipeline runs
questions.explanation: Deployment jobs with lifecycle hooks can trigger IaC scripts to create infrastructure in preDeploy, run tests in deploy, validate in postDeploy, and tear down in cleanup, enabling ephemeral test environments.

questions.question: What is a pipeline 'trigger' with a path filter in Azure Pipelines YAML?
options: A CI trigger that only runs the pipeline when changes are made to files in specified directory paths
A filter that prevents certain files from being deployed
A security policy for pipeline access
questions.explanation: Path filters on CI triggers allow pipelines to run only when files in specified directories are changed, preventing unnecessary builds when changes are in unrelated parts of the codebase.

questions.question: An organization wants to enforce that all Docker base images used in pipelines come from an approved internal registry, not from Docker Hub. How should they implement this?
options: Configure Azure Container Registry as the only allowed base image source via pipeline policies and use ACR Import to maintain approved images
Block Docker Hub at the network firewall level
Train developers to only use internal images
questions.explanation: Using ACR Import to maintain approved base images and pipeline policies to restrict base image sources ensures only approved, scanned images are used, reducing supply chain risk.

questions.question: What does 'CD' stand for in a CI/CD pipeline?
questions.explanation: CD stands for Continuous Delivery (automated release to a staging environment with manual production approval) or Continuous Deployment (automated release all the way to production).

questions.question: A pipeline deploys to Azure Kubernetes Service. The team wants to use a rolling update strategy that replaces pods gradually. Which Kubernetes deployment strategy should the pipeline configure?
options: RollingUpdate strategy with maxUnavailable and maxSurge settings
questions.explanation: The Kubernetes RollingUpdate strategy gradually replaces old pods with new ones, controlled by maxUnavailable (max pods down during update) and maxSurge (max extra pods during update) parameters.

questions.question: Which Azure service securely stores secrets, keys, and certificates for use in DevOps pipelines?
questions.explanation: Azure Key Vault securely stores and manages secrets, encryption keys, and certificates. DevOps pipelines can reference Key Vault secrets through variable groups for secure credential management.

questions.question: What type of identity should Azure Pipelines use to authenticate with Azure resources without storing credentials?
options: Managed Identity or Service Principal with a service connection
Shared admin password stored in a pipeline variable
Personal access token (PAT) of a team member
SSH key committed to the repository
questions.explanation: Managed Identities and Service Principals provide credential-free authentication for pipelines. Service connections in Azure DevOps securely manage the authentication without exposing secrets.

questions.question: Which tool scans project dependencies for known vulnerabilities in open-source components?
options: Software Composition Analysis (SCA) tools like Dependabot, Snyk, or WhiteSource
questions.explanation: SCA tools scan project dependencies against vulnerability databases (like NVD) to identify known security vulnerabilities in open-source libraries and recommend updated versions.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a 'secret variable' in Azure Pipelines?
options: A variable whose value is encrypted, masked in logs, and not available to scripts as environment variables by default
A variable that can only be accessed by administrators
A variable that stores Git credentials
A variable that is shared across all pipelines
questions.explanation: Secret variables in Azure Pipelines are encrypted at rest, masked in pipeline logs (replaced with ***), and require explicit mapping to be available as environment variables in scripts.

questions.question: What is a container vulnerability scan?
options: An analysis of container images for known security vulnerabilities in OS packages and application dependencies
A performance test for containerized applications
A network scan of container ports
A compliance check for container naming conventions
questions.explanation: Container vulnerability scanning analyzes Docker images for known CVEs in base OS packages, runtime libraries, and application dependencies, typically integrated into the CI/CD pipeline.

questions.question: What does SAST stand for in application security?
questions.explanation: Static Application Security Testing (SAST) analyzes source code, bytecode, or binary code for security vulnerabilities without executing the application, typically integrated early in the CI pipeline.

questions.question: Which Azure DevOps feature links a variable group to Azure Key Vault for automatic secret synchronization?
options: Variable groups with Key Vault integration
questions.explanation: Variable groups can be linked to an Azure Key Vault, automatically syncing secrets as pipeline variables. The pipeline accesses them through the variable group without storing secrets in Azure DevOps.

questions.question: A team needs to ensure all third-party libraries in their project use approved licenses (e.g., MIT, Apache 2.0). Which DevOps practice addresses this?
options: License compliance scanning integrated into the CI pipeline using tools like WhiteSource or FOSSA
Manual review of each package license
Only using packages with no license
Ignoring license requirements for internal projects
questions.explanation: License compliance scanning tools automatically identify licenses of all dependencies and flag packages with restrictive or unapproved licenses (like GPL in proprietary projects) during the CI pipeline.

questions.question: A DevOps team needs to rotate service principal credentials used in Azure Pipelines service connections without causing deployment failures. What is the recommended approach?
options: Use Workload Identity federation (OIDC) for service connections, which eliminates the need for credential rotation entirely
Update all service connections manually at the same time
Use long-lived credentials that never expire
Create new service principals each time and delete the old ones
questions.explanation: Workload Identity federation uses OpenID Connect (OIDC) tokens for authentication, eliminating the need for secret-based credentials and their rotation, providing the most secure and maintenance-free approach.

questions.question: A company needs to scan their application for vulnerabilities at runtime (not just in source code). Which type of security testing should they integrate into their CD pipeline?
questions.explanation: DAST tests the running application by sending requests and analyzing responses for vulnerabilities (XSS, SQL injection, etc.), complementing SAST which only analyzes source code.

questions.question: A team needs to prevent pipeline YAML files from being modified by unauthorized developers, as these files control deployments to production. Which protection should they implement?
options: Branch policies on the main branch requiring PR approval for changes to pipeline YAML files, plus required reviewers for pipeline paths
Store pipeline YAML in a separate secret repository
Use classic (GUI) pipelines instead of YAML
questions.explanation: Branch policies with PR approval requirements and path-based automatic reviewers for pipeline YAML files ensure that changes to deployment pipelines are reviewed by authorized personnel before merging.

questions.question: A security audit requires that all deployed container images must be signed and verified before deployment. Which technology provides container image signing?
options: Docker Content Trust (DCT) with Notary or Cosign for image signing and verification
Azure RBAC on the container registry
questions.explanation: Docker Content Trust (DCT) and Cosign (part of Sigstore) provide cryptographic signing and verification of container images, ensuring that only signed images from trusted sources are deployed.

questions.question: Which practice ensures that secrets are not accidentally exposed in pipeline logs during debugging?
options: Using issecret=true for custom logging variables and marking all sensitive variables as secret in the pipeline
Running pipelines in a private agent only
Using encrypted file storage for all logs
questions.explanation: Marking variables as secret (issecret=true) ensures their values are automatically masked (***) in pipeline logs. Custom logging should use the ##vso[task.setvariable] command with issecret=true.

questions.question: A team uses GitHub for source control and Azure Pipelines for CI/CD. They want automated dependency updates with vulnerability fixes. Which tool should they enable?
options: GitHub Dependabot for automated dependency update pull requests
questions.explanation: GitHub Dependabot automatically creates pull requests to update dependencies when new versions are available or when security vulnerabilities are detected, integrating with the CI pipeline for validation.

questions.question: A regulated company needs to ensure that every production deployment can be traced back to a specific code commit, approval, and test result. Which DevOps practice provides this?
options: End-to-end audit trail with linked work items, PR approvals, pipeline runs, and environment deployment history
questions.explanation: Azure DevOps provides an end-to-end audit trail by linking work items to PRs, PRs to builds, builds to releases, and releases to environment deployments, with full approval history.

questions.question: An organization wants to implement a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for their containerized applications. What should the pipeline generate?
options: An SBOM in SPDX or CycloneDX format listing all components, versions, and licenses in the container image
questions.explanation: An SBOM in standard formats (SPDX, CycloneDX) provides a complete inventory of software components, versions, and licenses in a container image, enabling vulnerability tracking and compliance verification.

questions.question: A DevOps team needs to implement the principle of least privilege for pipeline service connections. Production pipelines should only be able to deploy to production, not access development resources. How should this be configured?
options: Create separate service connections per environment with scoped RBAC, and restrict pipeline access using service connection security settings
Use a single admin service connection for all environments
Rely on pipeline YAML to limit which resources are accessed
Use personal access tokens scoped to each environment
questions.explanation: Separate service connections with environment-scoped RBAC roles, combined with pipeline-level access restrictions on service connections, enforce least privilege for deployments across environments.

questions.question: A team discovers that a critical vulnerability exists in a base Docker image used by all their microservices. They need to ensure all affected images are rebuilt and redeployed. Which automated approach handles this?
options: Configure Azure Container Registry Tasks to automatically trigger image rebuilds when base images are updated, coupled with pipeline-triggered redeployments
Wait for the next scheduled deployment cycle
Patch the running containers without rebuilding images
questions.explanation: ACR Tasks can trigger automatic rebuilds when base images are updated. Combined with container image update triggers in deployment pipelines, this creates a fully automated patching workflow.

questions.question: A security team wants to implement supply chain security for their CI/CD pipeline. They need to verify the integrity of all pipeline inputs (source code, dependencies, build tools). Which framework should they follow?
options: SLSA (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts) framework with provenance attestation
questions.explanation: SLSA (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts) provides a framework for increasing supply chain security through provenance attestation, build integrity verification, and source-to-artifact traceability.

questions.question: A DevOps engineer needs to ensure that pipeline runs can only use approved task versions and cannot install arbitrary marketplace extensions. Which Azure DevOps organizational setting controls this?
options: Organization-level extension management with approval workflows and task group restrictions
questions.explanation: Organization-level extension management allows admins to control which marketplace extensions can be installed, requiring approval for new extensions and restricting which tasks are available in pipelines.

questions.question: A company needs to detect secrets that have already been committed to their Git history. Which tool and approach should they use?
options: Run a full repository history scan using tools like truffleHog or GitLeaks, then rotate any discovered secrets immediately
questions.explanation: Tools like truffleHog and GitLeaks scan the entire Git history (all commits, all branches) using regex patterns and entropy analysis to detect committed secrets, which must then be rotated immediately.

questions.question: A team needs to enforce that all deployed Kubernetes pods run with read-only root filesystems and non-root users. Which Kubernetes security mechanism enforces this?
options: Pod Security Standards (PSS) with Restricted profile enforced via Pod Security Admission or OPA Gatekeeper
questions.explanation: Pod Security Standards (Restricted profile) enforced through Pod Security Admission or OPA Gatekeeper policies can mandate read-only root filesystems, non-root users, and other security constraints on all pods.

questions.question: An organization needs to implement a break-glass procedure for emergency production deployments that bypasses normal approval gates while maintaining an audit trail. How should this be configured?
options: Create a separate emergency pipeline with reduced gates, restricted to a security group, with mandatory post-deployment audit review
Give all developers production deployment access
Use the Azure DevOps REST API to bypass approvals
questions.explanation: A separate emergency pipeline with reduced approval requirements, restricted access via security group membership, and mandatory post-deployment audit review balances rapid response with accountability.

questions.question: Which GitHub feature provides automated code scanning, secret scanning, and dependency review as part of the development workflow?
questions.explanation: GitHub Advanced Security provides CodeQL-based code scanning (SAST), secret scanning (detecting committed credentials), and dependency review (identifying vulnerable dependencies in PRs).

questions.question: A DevOps team wants to prevent container images with critical vulnerabilities from being deployed to production. Which pipeline configuration achieves this?
options: Add a container image scanning step (e.g., Trivy, Aqua) that fails the pipeline if critical CVEs are found
Rely on the container runtime to block vulnerable images
questions.explanation: Integrating a container scanning tool (Trivy, Aqua, Snyk Container) as a pipeline gate that fails on critical vulnerabilities prevents vulnerable images from progressing to production deployment.

questions.question: A team needs to implement mutual TLS authentication between their Azure Pipelines self-hosted agents and the deployment targets. What infrastructure is required?
options: A PKI infrastructure with client certificates on agents and server certificates on targets, with a shared CA for trust validation
questions.explanation: Mutual TLS requires both client (agent) and server (target) to present certificates signed by a trusted CA, providing strong bidirectional authentication and encrypted communication.

questions.question: What is credential scanning in the context of DevOps security?
options: Automated detection of hardcoded secrets, API keys, and passwords in source code and configuration files
Scanning employee badges for physical access
Testing login pages for authentication vulnerabilities
Reviewing access control lists on Azure resources
questions.explanation: Credential scanning automatically detects hardcoded secrets (API keys, passwords, connection strings, tokens) in source code and configuration files, preventing accidental exposure in repositories.

questions.question: A DevOps team needs to ensure their IaC (Terraform/ARM) templates follow security best practices before deployment. Which type of scanning should be integrated into the pipeline?
options: IaC security scanning using tools like Checkov, tfsec, or PSRule that analyze templates for misconfigurations
questions.explanation: IaC security scanning tools (Checkov, tfsec, PSRule) analyze infrastructure templates before deployment, detecting misconfigurations like public storage buckets, overly permissive security groups, or missing encryption.

questions.question: A company wants to restrict which Azure Pipelines can access their production service connections. How should they configure access control?
options: Use pipeline permissions on the service connection to authorize only specific production pipelines
Share the service connection with all pipelines in the organization
Create a separate Azure DevOps organization for production
Use a single service connection for all environments
questions.explanation: Service connection security settings allow administrators to restrict which pipelines can use the connection, ensuring only authorized production pipelines can access production resources.

questions.question: What is the difference between SAST and DAST in a DevOps security pipeline?
options: SAST analyzes source code without executing it (shift-left), while DAST tests the running application by sending requests (shift-right)
SAST is faster but less accurate than DAST
DAST can only find SQL injection vulnerabilities
They are the same tool with different names
questions.explanation: SAST (white-box) analyzes source code early in development to find vulnerabilities like buffer overflows and injection flaws. DAST (black-box) tests the running application to find runtime vulnerabilities.

questions.question: Which Azure service provides application performance monitoring (APM) for web applications?
questions.explanation: Application Insights is an APM service that monitors live web applications, automatically detecting performance anomalies, and providing powerful analytics tools for diagnosing issues.

questions.question: What does Application Insights use to track requests across distributed microservices?
questions.explanation: Application Insights uses distributed tracing with W3C TraceContext correlation IDs to track requests across multiple microservices, enabling end-to-end transaction visibility.

questions.question: Which Azure Monitor feature stores and queries log data using Kusto Query Language (KQL)?
questions.explanation: Log Analytics workspace is the central data store for Azure Monitor logs, supporting rich querying with Kusto Query Language (KQL) for analysis, visualization, and alerting.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of Azure Monitor?
options: To collect, analyze, and act on telemetry from Azure and on-premises environments
questions.explanation: Azure Monitor is a comprehensive monitoring solution that collects, analyzes, and acts on telemetry data from cloud and on-premises environments, maximizing availability and performance.

questions.question: What is the purpose of an Azure Monitor alert rule?
options: To evaluate conditions on metrics or logs and trigger notifications or actions when conditions are met
questions.explanation: Alert rules evaluate conditions (metric thresholds, log query results) and trigger action groups (email, SMS, webhook, Azure Function, Logic App) when those conditions are met.

questions.question: What are the four golden signals of monitoring as defined by Google SRE?
options: Uptime, Response Time, Error Rate, and Cost
questions.explanation: The four golden signals are Latency (time to service a request), Traffic (demand on the system), Errors (rate of failed requests), and Saturation (how full the service is).

questions.question: Which Application Insights feature automatically detects performance anomalies without manual configuration?
questions.explanation: Smart Detection in Application Insights uses machine learning to automatically detect performance anomalies, failure rate changes, and other unusual patterns without requiring manual threshold configuration.

questions.question: What type of data does Azure Monitor Metrics collect?
options: Numeric time-series data at regular intervals (like CPU percentage, request count, memory usage)
questions.explanation: Azure Monitor Metrics collects lightweight numeric time-series data at regular intervals, such as CPU percentage, request counts, memory usage, and response times.

questions.question: A team needs to track how users interact with their web application, including page views, button clicks, and user journeys. Which Application Insights feature provides this?
options: Usage analytics (Users, Sessions, Events, Funnels, User Flows)
questions.explanation: Application Insights Usage analytics provides tools like Users, Sessions, Events, Funnels, and User Flows to understand how users interact with the application and where they drop off.

questions.question: A DevOps team needs to detect and alert on a sudden increase in HTTP 500 errors from their API. Which Azure Monitor feature should they configure?
options: A metric alert rule on the HTTP 5xx error count with dynamic thresholds
A scheduled log query running daily
questions.explanation: Metric alert rules with dynamic thresholds use machine learning to automatically determine normal baselines and alert when metrics deviate significantly, detecting sudden error rate increases.

questions.question: A team needs to monitor the availability of their web application from multiple geographic locations worldwide. Which Application Insights feature provides this?
options: Availability tests (URL ping tests and multi-step web tests)
questions.explanation: Availability tests send requests to your application from multiple Azure locations worldwide, checking for proper responses and alerting when availability drops below thresholds.

questions.question: Which Application Insights feature provides a visual topology of the application showing dependencies between components and their health status?
questions.explanation: Application Map provides a visual topology of the application, showing all components (services, databases, external calls), their dependencies, and health indicators (failure rates, response times).

questions.question: A DevOps team needs to centralize logs from Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) pods, including stdout/stderr output. Which monitoring solution should they configure?
options: Azure Monitor Container Insights with Log Analytics workspace integration
Azure Blob Storage with diagnostic settings
questions.explanation: Container Insights collects container logs (stdout/stderr), performance metrics, and Kubernetes events from AKS clusters, storing them in a Log Analytics workspace for querying and alerting.

questions.question: A team wants to define reliability targets for their service. They agree that 99.9% of API requests should complete in under 200ms. What is this type of target called?
questions.explanation: A Service Level Objective (SLO) is an internal target value for a service level. The SLI is the measurement (latency), while the SLO is the target (99.9% under 200ms). An SLA is a formal customer agreement.

questions.question: A developer needs to see real-time telemetry (live request rate, failure rate, dependency calls) while debugging an issue in production. Which Application Insights feature should they use?
questions.explanation: Live Metrics Stream provides real-time telemetry with approximately 1-second latency, showing incoming requests, outgoing dependencies, failures, and resource utilization without the normal ingestion delay.

questions.question: A team needs to configure alerts that trigger when no data is received from a monitored application for 5 minutes (indicating the application may be down). Which alert type handles this?
options: Metric alert with 'no data' condition configured
Log alert with a query for null results
questions.explanation: Metric alerts can be configured with a 'no data' action that triggers when expected metric data stops arriving, indicating the monitored resource may be unreachable or down.

questions.question: What is the purpose of an error budget in Site Reliability Engineering (SRE)?
options: The allowed amount of unreliability based on the SLO, used to balance feature velocity with reliability
The financial budget allocated for fixing bugs
The maximum number of errors allowed per deployment
The time allocated for error investigation
questions.explanation: An error budget (100% - SLO = error budget) defines how much unreliability is tolerable. When the error budget is consumed, the team prioritizes reliability over new features.

questions.question: Which Application Insights feature helps identify the root cause of performance bottlenecks by analyzing CPU and memory allocation at the code level?
questions.explanation: Application Insights Profiler captures performance traces of the running application, showing hot paths in the code, CPU allocation, and execution timing to pinpoint performance bottlenecks.

questions.question: A DevOps team needs to implement a comprehensive monitoring strategy that includes infrastructure metrics, application logs, distributed traces, and synthetic monitoring. Which combination of Azure Monitor features provides full-stack observability?
options: Azure Monitor Metrics + Log Analytics + Application Insights (with distributed tracing) + Availability Tests
questions.explanation: Full-stack observability combines infrastructure metrics (Azure Monitor), log analytics (Log Analytics), application tracing (Application Insights with distributed tracing), and synthetic monitoring (Availability Tests).

questions.question: A team needs to implement canary analysis that automatically compares metrics between the canary deployment and the stable production deployment, failing the rollout if the canary shows degraded performance. Which approach should they implement?
options: Automated canary analysis using Application Insights metrics queries in a pipeline gate that compares canary vs. baseline error rates and latency
Manual comparison of dashboards during deployment
Load testing the canary environment separately
questions.explanation: Automated canary analysis uses Application Insights queries in pipeline gates to statistically compare canary metrics (error rate, latency, saturation) against the baseline, automatically promoting or rolling back.

questions.question: A DevOps team needs to reduce Application Insights costs for a high-traffic application while retaining the ability to investigate individual issues. Which data reduction strategy should they use?
options: Sampling (adaptive or fixed-rate) that reduces telemetry volume while maintaining statistically representative data
Disable Application Insights during peak hours
questions.explanation: Adaptive sampling automatically adjusts the sampling rate based on telemetry volume, reducing data ingestion costs while maintaining statistically representative data. Individual traces are preserved for investigation.

questions.question: An architect needs to design a logging strategy where different log levels are routed to different destinations: debug logs to cheap cold storage, error logs to real-time alerting, and audit logs to a compliance data store. Which Azure Monitor feature enables this?
options: Diagnostic settings with multiple destinations and Data Collection Rules (DCRs) for filtering and routing
A single Log Analytics workspace for everything
Azure Event Hubs as the sole destination
questions.explanation: Data Collection Rules (DCRs) enable filtering and routing of telemetry to different destinations based on log level, namespace, or other criteria, optimizing cost and compliance requirements.

questions.question: A team wants to implement chaos engineering to test their monitoring and alerting systems. They plan to inject failures and verify that alerts fire correctly. Which Azure service supports this?
options: Azure Chaos Studio with experiments that inject faults and validate monitoring responses
questions.explanation: Azure Chaos Studio provides managed chaos engineering experiments that inject faults (CPU pressure, network delay, pod kill) into Azure resources, allowing teams to validate that monitoring and alerting respond correctly.

questions.question: A team needs to trace a single user request through 8 microservices deployed across Azure Kubernetes Service, Azure Functions, and Azure Service Bus. Which Application Insights capability enables this end-to-end correlation?
options: W3C Trace Context propagation with Application Insights SDKs or auto-instrumentation across all services
Custom header propagation without SDK support
questions.explanation: W3C Trace Context (traceparent header) propagated by Application Insights SDKs or auto-instrumentation enables end-to-end distributed tracing across all services, correlating the full transaction chain.

questions.question: A team needs to create KQL queries that correlate Application Insights request data with Azure Kubernetes container logs to diagnose pod-level issues affecting API performance. Which approach enables this?
options: Send both Application Insights and Container Insights data to the same Log Analytics workspace, then use cross-table KQL queries joining requests with ContainerLog
Manually compare data from two separate dashboards
Export both data sources to CSV and merge in Excel
questions.explanation: When Application Insights and Container Insights share a Log Analytics workspace, KQL queries can join request telemetry with container logs, correlating application performance with pod-level diagnostics.

questions.question: A DevOps team needs to implement SLO-based alerting. Instead of alerting on every error, they want to alert only when the error budget burn rate threatens to exhaust the monthly error budget. Which approach should they implement?
options: Multi-window, multi-burn-rate alerts that fire when the error budget consumption rate exceeds defined thresholds over multiple time windows
A simple threshold alert on error count
A daily email report on error budget status
Alert only at the end of the month if the SLO is missed
questions.explanation: Multi-window, multi-burn-rate alerting (from the Google SRE Workbook) fires when the error budget burn rate is unsustainable across both short and long time windows, providing timely yet noise-free alerts.

questions.question: A company is migrating from a third-party APM tool to Application Insights. They have custom metrics and dashboards they want to preserve. Which Application Insights feature allows sending custom business metrics?
options: Custom metrics API (TelemetryClient.TrackMetric and TrackEvent) with custom dimensions
questions.explanation: Application Insights SDK provides TrackMetric, TrackEvent, and other custom telemetry methods that allow sending business-specific metrics with custom dimensions for querying and visualization.

questions.question: A team needs their Azure Monitor alerts to automatically create incidents in ServiceNow and page on-call engineers via PagerDuty simultaneously. How should the action group be configured?
options: Configure an action group with both an ITSM Connector (ServiceNow) and a webhook action (PagerDuty) that trigger simultaneously on alert
Create two separate alert rules with different action groups
Use Azure Logic Apps to fan out notifications
Configure email alerts and manually create tickets
questions.explanation: A single Azure Monitor action group supports multiple action types simultaneously. ITSM Connector for ServiceNow and webhook for PagerDuty can both be configured in the same action group.

questions.question: A team is using Grafana dashboards for visualization. They want to connect Grafana to Azure Monitor data sources. Which approach provides native integration?
options: Azure Managed Grafana with built-in Azure Monitor and Azure Data Explorer data source plugins
Export data to CSV and import into Grafana
Screenshot Azure dashboards and embed in Grafana
questions.explanation: Azure Managed Grafana provides a fully managed Grafana instance with native Azure Monitor, Log Analytics, and Azure Data Explorer data source plugins, enabling rich dashboards on Azure telemetry data.

questions.question: Which Application Insights feature allows you to capture a snapshot of the call stack and local variables when an exception occurs in production?
questions.explanation: Snapshot Debugger automatically captures debug snapshots when exceptions occur in production .NET applications, showing the call stack and local variable values at the point of failure without impacting performance.
